text: The surgeon identifies and transects the round ligament, a support structure with visible blood vessels. Following this, the bladder is carefully separated from the uterus, creating a bladder flap.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to remove the fallopian tubes, which are visibly detached from the uterus. The ovaries are left intact to prevent hormonal changes.

The surgeon disconnects the ovary from the uterus by cutting the utero-ovarian ligament, while the ovary remains attached to the pelvis with its main blood supply intact via the infundibulopelvic ligament.

The surgeon identifies the thick uterine artery, which serves as the main blood supply to the uterus. With the blood supply controlled, the surgeon prepares to disconnect the uterus from surrounding structures.
A firm cap is placed at the cervico-vaginal junction, serving as a guide for the upcoming dissection during the abdominal portion of the hysterectomy.
The surgeon performs delicate suturing to close the vaginal cuff following the removal of the uterus.
